rationale: Rotation creates the physical context in which gravity-sensing cells may respond.
Shoot reorientation may engage gravity-sensing tissues such as the endodermis.
A change in orientation is expected to promote amyloplast sedimentation relative to the gravity vector.
Statocytes are placed upstream of the sedimentation process in this seed model.
The endodermis is represented as a tissue context for shoot amyloplast-based sensing.
Sedimentation is hypothesised to feed into LAZY/LZY-dependent signalling.
The LAZY/LZY module is hypothesised to influence PIN-mediated auxin transport polarity.
PIN-mediated redistribution is hypothesised to generate asymmetric auxin levels.
Auxin asymmetry is hypothesised to promote unequal cell expansion across the shoot.
Unequal cell expansion is expected to promote curvature.
Curvature is treated as the physical component of time-dependent gravitropic bending.
Genetic background may alter the strength or behaviour of gravity signalling modules.
Genetic background may alter auxin transport and redistribution during bending.
Genetic background may alter growth capacity or asymmetry.
Comparative gravitropic categories may summarize inherited differences among families or genotypes.
Family-level differences may be associated with different bending trajectories over time.
